abstract:
This book provides a systematic analysis of the Russia-Ukraine war, employing the framework of "resilient fighting power" to evaluate the operational performance of both belligerents during the first year of the full-scale invasion. While the broader conflict began in 2014, the escalation on February 24, 2022, reignited global debates on high-intensity, inter-state conventional warfare—a phenomenon not witnessed in Europe for decades. 

Rather than attempting an exhaustive military history, this work offers a preliminary cross-domain assessment of how each side’s fighting power evolved and performed in the heat of combat. The analysis begins by tracing the conceptual foundations of both the Ukrainian and Russian Armed Forces, examining how they have adapted or broken away from inherited Soviet strategic traditions in the post-Cold War era. 

The study then provides a detailed evaluation of warfighting across the land, air, maritime, and cyber domains. It explores the critical role of international allied support, including the impact of sanctions and the delivery of Western weaponry, in bolstering the resilience of the Ukrainian resistance. The book concludes by reflecting on the place of inter-state conflict within the current global strategic environment and its implications for the future of warfare. This text will be a valuable resource for students and scholars of military and strategic studies, defense policy, international relations, and Russian and Eastern European affairs.
